mentalModels.name: OODA Loop
mentalModels.description: A four-stage iterative cycle for making rapid decisions in dynamic environments: Observe, Orient, Decide, Act.
mentalModels.analogy: Like a point guard in basketball observing the defense, orienting based on plays, deciding to pass or shoot, and acting.
mentalModels.useCase: During a sales negotiation, when a prospect raises a budget constraint, use the loop to observe their reaction, orient your strategy, decide on a response, and act.

mentalModels.name: First-Principles Reasoning
mentalModels.description: Breaking down a complex problem into its most basic, fundamental truths and then reasoning up from there.
mentalModels.analogy: Like a master chef who invents new dishes by understanding the fundamental properties of ingredients, not just by following recipes.
mentalModels.useCase: Vetting a new business idea by analyzing the core problem and fundamental costs, rather than just copying competitor pricing.

mentalModels.name: Second-Order Thinking
mentalModels.description: Considering the subsequent, indirect consequences of an action, not just the immediate effects.
mentalModels.analogy: Like a chess master planning several moves ahead, not just the next one.
mentalModels.useCase: Evaluating the long-term impact of offering a steep discount, such as devaluing the product and attracting high-churn customers.

mentalModels.name: 5 Whys
mentalModels.description: An iterative root cause analysis technique to explore the cause-and-effect relationships by repeatedly asking 'Why?'
mentalModels.analogy: Like a curious child who keeps asking 'Why?' until they reach a foundational answer.
mentalModels.useCase: Discovering a prospect's hesitation is not about the timeline, but about their need to secure funding.

mentalModels.name: Pareto Principle
mentalModels.description: The principle that roughly 80% of the effects come from 20% of the causes.
mentalModels.analogy: In a wardrobe, you wear 20% of your clothes 80% of the time.
mentalModels.useCase: Focusing sales efforts on the 20% of clients who generate 80% of the revenue.

mentalModels.name: Inversion
mentalModels.description: A problem-solving technique where you consider how to achieve the opposite of your goal, then work to avoid those pitfalls.
mentalModels.analogy: To have a great day, first ask 'What would ruin my day?' and then avoid those things.
mentalModels.useCase: To launch a successful business, first ask 'What would guarantee failure?' and build strategies to prevent those modes.

mentalModels.name: Circle of Competence
mentalModels.description: The specific area(s) where you possess genuine deep knowledge and expertise. Operating within it leads to better decisions.
mentalModels.analogy: A baseball hitter who only swings at pitches in their high-average 'strike zone'.
mentalModels.useCase: Selecting a business idea aligned with your existing industry knowledge and skills to increase the odds of success.

mentalModels.name: Eisenhower Matrix
mentalModels.description: A task management tool that prioritizes activities by urgency and importance into four quadrants.
mentalModels.analogy: Sorting daily mail: an overdue bill is 'Do First,' a minor request is 'Delegate,' junk mail is 'Delete'.
mentalModels.useCase: A sales professional uses it to prioritize preparing a client presentation (Do), strategic prospecting (Schedule), and data entry (Delegate).

mentalModels.name: Hanlon's Razor
mentalModels.description: Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity, incompetence, or circumstance.
mentalModels.analogy: If someone cuts you off in traffic, consider they might be distracted or rushing, not malicious.
mentalModels.useCase: If a prospect doesn't respond, assume they are busy or waiting for approvals, not ignoring you.

mentalModels.name: Regret Minimization
mentalModels.description: Evaluating options by projecting your potential regret in 10 minutes, 10 months, and 10 years.
mentalModels.analogy: Time-traveling to your 80-year-old self and asking which path you'd regret not taking.
mentalModels.useCase: Deciding whether to leave a stable job for a startup by considering long-term fulfillment over short-term anxiety.

mentalModels.name: 'Systems' vs. Goals
mentalModels.description: Focusing on building reliable systems and habits rather than obsessing over specific outcomes.
mentalModels.analogy: A goal is a target; a system is practicing daily. The system ensures you eventually hit targets.
mentalModels.useCase: Instead of 'get a new job' implement a system: 'apply to one quality job and contact one person on LinkedIn every morning'.

mentalModels.name: Occam’s Razor
mentalModels.description: The simplest explanation or solution is usually the best, all else being equal.
mentalModels.analogy: If you hear hoofbeats, think horses, not zebras.
mentalModels.useCase: When diagnosing a product issue, check for simple causes before assuming a complex system glitch.

mentalModels.name: Circle of Control
mentalModels.description: Focusing energy on things you can control or influence, and not on things you can't.
mentalModels.analogy: You can't control the weather, but you can control whether you bring an umbrella.
mentalModels.useCase: In sales, you can't control the economy, but you can control your prospecting activity and pitch quality.

mentalModels.name: Opportunity Cost
mentalModels.description: The implicit cost of forgoing the next best alternative when making a choice.
mentalModels.analogy: The opportunity cost of watching Netflix is the book you didn't read or the workout you skipped.
mentalModels.useCase: Pursuing a low-value sales lead consumes time that could have been spent on a hotter prospect.

mentalModels.name: Rapid Experimentation
mentalModels.description: Instead of over-planning, quickly test a 'Minimum Viable Product' to gather feedback early.
mentalModels.analogy: Cooking a small sample of a new recipe to taste and adjust before making a huge batch.
mentalModels.useCase: Building a simple landing page to gauge interest in a business idea before investing heavily in development.
